title: Emotions are God-given signals, not shameful threats

sections.label: What emotions do
bullets: Emotions reveal what we value, fear, hope for, and need; they surface reality in the heart.
Emotions fuel behavior even when we think we are being purely rational; they often operate beneath awareness.
Naming emotions is not indulgence; it is truthful self-awareness that supports repentance, repair, and intimacy.

sections.title: What love really is: personhood, boundaries, and truthful pattern recognition

sections.label: Love honors personhood
bullets: Love does not erase individuality or collapse two people into one undifferentiated emotional unit.
Love respects another person's needs, pace, limits, conscience, and distinct inner world.
To love someone is not to absorb them, control them, or demand total emotional sameness.

sections.label: Why enmeshment is harmful
bullets: Enmeshment confuses closeness with lack of boundaries.
It can pressure people to over-function emotionally, carry what is not theirs, or lose clarity about what behavior actually happened.
Healthy love includes differentiation: connection with clear respect for individuality and limits.

sections.label: How this tool helps with real relationships
bullets: This tool helps users log their emotional responses to behaviors over time rather than relying only on accusation or vague memory.
It helps identify which recurring behaviors most consistently affect safety, trust, dignity, or clarity.
It can also clarify when a partner may not be responding with the empathy, repair, or respect the situation requires.

sections.label: When boundaries may be needed
bullets: If repeated harmful patterns continue and honest repair does not follow, the pattern itself may be signaling the need for personal protective boundaries.
Boundaries are not a failure of love; they can be a form of stewardship, truthfulness, and protection.
The goal is to observe behavior honestly, name impact clearly, and respond in reality rather than wishful thinking.

scriptures.principle: Love does no wrong to a neighbor.
Love pays attention to the other person's good and reality.
We bear burdens, but we do not erase personal responsibility or boundaries.
Love is not self-seeking and does not force itself onto another person.

sections.label: What happens when we ignore them
bullets: Unprocessed emotions often leak out as sarcasm, control, withdrawal, contempt, blame, or emotional numbness.
Ignoring emotions can delay repair and multiply harm across time, especially in close relationships.

scriptures.principle: Weep with those who weep; emotional reality is part of faithful presence.
Be quick to hear, slow to speak; emotional restraint and listening protect relationship.
A crushed spirit impacts the body; inner life matters to whole-person health.
The heart responds with joy and praise; emotions can become worship when directed toward God.

title: Nonviolent Communication helps us speak truth in love

sections.label: The NVC frame (practical structure)
bullets: Observation: describe what happened without mind-reading or labels.
Feeling: name your internal experience without blame.
Need: identify what value/need is impacted.
Request: ask concretely for a change or repair step.

sections.label: Why this matters spiritually
bullets: Truth without love becomes condemnation; love without truth becomes avoidance.
This tool is designed to convert reactive speech into clarity, accountability, and respect.

scriptures.principle: Speak the truth in love; truth and love belong together.
Speak what builds up; avoid corrupt talk that harms.
A gentle answer turns away wrath; tone can prevent escalation.
Speak evil of no one; avoid quarreling; be gentle and show courtesy.

title: Christians are called to a higher standard: restore, edify, and set boundaries when needed

sections.label: Accountability is love when it restores
bullets: Correction is meant to heal and restore, not to dominate, humiliate, or control.
Repair requires humility, specificity, and self-watchfulness (so correction does not become pride).

sections.label: When boundaries are necessary
bullets: If someone persists in divisive or harmful behavior after clear warnings, boundaries can be appropriate.
Boundaries are not hatred; they can be protection and stewardship when repair is refused.

scriptures.principle: Restore someone caught in sin with gentleness; keep watch on yourself.
Warn a divisive person, then set a boundary if they persist.
Bear with weaknesses and build up your neighbor for their good.
God calls us to holiness; disregarding holiness is disregarding God.

title: Needs are universal; love shows up as respect, stewardship, and mutual care

sections.label: Needs language (human reality)
bullets: Every person has needs (safety, clarity, rest, belonging, agency, honesty, stability, etc.).
Maturity includes learning to meet needs responsibly rather than extracting them through coercion or manipulation.

sections.label: Burdens vs. loads (mutual care with responsibility)
bullets: We help carry burdens: needs someone cannot carry alone.
We also bear our own load: personal responsibility and ownership remain.

sections.label: Respect is love in action
bullets: Respect means honoring limits, pace, and personhood.
Ignoring boundaries is not love; it is harm dressed up as urgency.

scriptures.principle: Bear one another’s burdens, yet each bears their own load.
Love fulfills the law and does no wrong to a neighbor.
In humility count others more significant; look to their interests.
Seek the good of your neighbor, not only your own.

title: Renewing the mind: noticing emotions, then reshaping attention toward truth and love

sections.label: Renewal is not denial
bullets: We renew the mind by bringing inner experience into truth, not by suppressing it.
Emotional awareness helps identify distortions and temptations early, before they become patterns.

sections.label: Focus is a discipline
bullets: Attention can be trained toward what is true, noble, pure, lovely, and admirable.
Gratitude is a replacement practice that interrupts anxiety loops and strengthens peace.

scriptures.principle: Be transformed by the renewing of your mind; offer yourself to God.
Think on what is true, noble, right, pure, lovely, and admirable.
Prayer with thanksgiving replaces anxiety; God’s peace guards hearts and minds.
Let Christ’s peace rule in your heart; be thankful.

title: Naming toxic patterns matters, but violent confrontation multiplies harm

sections.label: Why naming harm matters
bullets: Toxic behaviors (contempt, manipulation, stonewalling, blame-shifting, deception) fracture trust and safety.
Calling sin “sin” can be necessary, but must not become identity attack or condemnation.

sections.label: Why violent call-outs fail
bullets: Accusation and hostility usually produce defensiveness, escalation, or shutdown—blocking repair.
Nonviolent clarity increases the chance of understanding, accountability, and change.

sections.label: How this app supports both moments
bullets: Nonviolent lane: speak with love and clarity (observation → feeling → need → request).
Violent lane: discharge intensity safely, then redirect toward accountability and repair steps.

scriptures.principle: Avoid quarreling; be gentle and courteous.
Speak what builds up; avoid corrupting talk.
Love does no wrong to a neighbor.
Conflicts often arise from disordered desires; surface motivations matter.

title: We are accountable for our words: honesty, gentleness, and repair

sections.label: Speech is never neutral
bullets: Words can heal or fracture; tone, timing, and truthfulness matter.
This tool reduces exaggeration, mind-reading, contempt, and spiritualized deflection.

sections.label: Truthfulness protects shared reality
bullets: Honesty includes clarity, confession, and quick repair—not image management.
Promises and follow-through create safety; silence and omission can create confusion and fear.

scriptures.principle: We will give account for careless words; speech matters before God.
Let your yes be yes; integrity in speech matters.
Put away falsehood; speak truth with your neighbor.
Faithfulness delights the Lord; lying is opposed to God’s character.

title: Freedom from sinful patterns comes through love and Spirit-led transformation

sections.label: Why “try harder” often fails
bullets: Shame-driven striving often cycles back into relapse and hopelessness.
Many compulsions are counterfeit substitutes for love (your notes emphasize lust/addiction as false love).

sections.label: How God changes people
bullets: Transformation is Spirit-led and progressive—one degree of glory to another.
Love fulfills the law; love is the environment where real repentance and freedom grow.

sections.label: Love summarizes the whole command
bullets: Jesus summarized the law as loving God and loving neighbor.
This tool is meant to make that love practical in real conversations: truthful, respectful, accountable.

scriptures.principle: Where the Spirit is, there is freedom; transformation happens over time.
Love fulfills the law; love does no wrong to a neighbor.
Self-sacrificing love chooses service for others’ good.
God’s love is active: He rejoices, quiets, and steadies His people.

title: Jesus corrects toward repentance: actions and fruit, not identity attacks

sections.label: How Jesus often spoke to individuals
bullets: Jesus regularly named concrete truth and then called people toward repentance, faith, worship, or restored obedience rather than trapping them inside a condemning identity label.
His pattern with individuals was often exposure plus invitation: truth is brought into the light so the person can turn and live differently.

sections.label: Where the strongest rebukes were aimed
bullets: Jesus’ sharpest public denunciations were often aimed at religious leaders who burdened others, blocked access to God, and used holiness language without love or integrity.
That makes His rebukes a warning against institutional hypocrisy and spiritual obstruction, not a license for everyday contempt in personal conflict.

sections.label: What this means for communication here
bullets: Behavior-based correction sounds like: 'You said X, but Y happened' or 'This action harmed trust.'
Identity attack sounds like: 'You are a liar' or 'You are evil.' This often produces shame, defensiveness, and collapse instead of honest repair.

scriptures.principle: Jesus rebukes leaders who burden others, shut the kingdom, and practice outward righteousness without inward integrity.
Jesus tells the truth to the Samaritan woman and leads her toward worship and revelation, not mere humiliation.
Jesus refuses condemnation while still saying, 'Go, and from now on sin no more.'
Jesus’ nearness leads Zacchaeus toward repentance, restitution, and changed behavior.

title: Thoughts, emotions, words, and actions are spiritually connected

sections.label: The inner life becomes outward life
bullets: Thought patterns shape emotional responses; emotions influence tone, attention, and impulse; repeated words and actions become relational patterns.
Because of that, communication repair is not only about phrasing better sentences. It is also about examining what is being rehearsed in the mind and cherished in the heart.

sections.label: Why early attention matters
bullets: When distortion is caught at the thought level, it is easier to prevent exaggeration, accusation, contempt, panic, or withdrawal later in the chain.
Renewal does not deny emotion; it brings thought and emotion under truth so speech and action can become more loving and more responsible.

scriptures.principle: Transformation happens through renewal of the mind.
Take thoughts captive to obey Christ rather than letting them rule unchecked.
Inner desire, when cultivated wrongly, grows outward into sin.
Words and defilement come out from the heart.

title: Emotions are signals, not sovereign justification

sections.label: A necessary caution
bullets: Some emotional frameworks help people name experience, but can drift toward treating the self as final authority and emotion as automatic moral permission.
Biblically, emotion is real and important, but it does not excuse sin, erase responsibility, or make blame-shifting righteous.

sections.label: Radical responsibility before God
bullets: The mature question is not only 'What do I feel?' but also 'What am I doing with what I feel?'
Owning feelings and needs should move a person toward truthful prayer, honest confession, wise boundaries, and loving speech rather than control, accusation, or self-excusing narratives.

scriptures.principle: Each person bears their own load; responsibility is not dissolved by emotion.
Human wrath does not produce God’s righteousness; strong feeling must still be governed.
Emotion must not become sin, and speech must still build up (edify) and give grace.
Love does not center the self alone, but also attends to the other person’s good.
